title: Arm CPUs Take Number 1 in Latest Top500 List with Chinese LineShine
gist: China's LineShine supercomputer took the #1 Top500 spot at ~2.2 ExaFLOPS Linpack (22% faster than El Capitan), and is notably an all-CPU Arm system with no GPUs, built on Huawei-designed LX2 CPUs with on-chip HBM and 800Gbps NICs. It displaces AMD from the top for the first time in 4 years, demonstrating a Chinese sovereign HPC path around GPU sanctions.

insights.claim: China's LineShine supercomputer from NSCS Shenzhen took the #1 Top500 spot at just under 2.2 ExaFLOPS on Linpack, roughly 22% faster than El Capitan, and is the first system to publicly surpass 2 ExaFLOPS.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: China now publicly leads global supercomputing, a sovereignty and geopolitical compute milestone.
insights.quote: the first to publicly surpass 2 ExaFLOPS on Linpack.
insights.locator: In a big win for Arm computing

insights.claim: Unlike GPU-driven El Capitan and Frontier, LineShine is a pure CPU system with no GPUs, relying on Armv9 cores with SVE and SME vector and matrix accelerator blocks.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Shows a viable GPU-free HPC architecture, relevant amid US GPU export controls on China.
insights.quote: LineShine is purely a CPU system – there are no GPUs to be found here.

insights.claim: The LX2 CPU, thought to be designed by Huawei, comprises two compute dies with onboard HBM, each die holding 152 Armv9 cores, totaling 304 cores, 32GB HBM, and 256GB DDR5 per chip across eight NUMA domains.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Demonstrates Huawei's advanced domestic CPU and HBM integration capability despite sanctions.
insights.locator: The fundamental building block of LineShine is the LX2 CPU.

insights.claim: Each LX2 processor consumes 690 Watts, GPU-like power, and integrates 800Gbps NICs on chip, a level of integration not widely seen since Intel Xeon with OmniPath.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: On-chip 800Gbps networking signals aggressive vertical integration in Chinese HPC silicon.
insights.quote: each LX2 processor consumes 690 Watts of power.
insights.locator: With NSCS aiming for GPU-like performance

insights.claim: LineShine sustained 2.2 EFLOPs versus a 2.74 EFLOPs theoretical peak (80%), beating US exascale GPU systems that run 50-65% of peak, but at 42MW and only ~52 GFLOPs/watt versus El Capitan's 60 GFLOPs/watt.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: CPU system wins on sustained-vs-peak efficiency but loses on energy efficiency, the expected CPU-vs-GPU tradeoff.
insights.quote: the official Top500 submission for LineShine has it listed as consuming 42MW of power, for about 52 GFLOPs/watt.
insights.locator: Still, the entire system is reported to be very power-hungry.

insights.claim: For the first time in 4 years an AMD-powered system is no longer #1, with El Capitan (MI300A) and Frontier dropping to #2 and #3; AMD still powers 4 of the top 10, NVIDIA has three Hopper systems, and Intel's Aurora is the sole Intel GPU system at #4.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Competitive HPC vendor share map; AMD loses symbolic top spot but retains broad top-10 presence.
insights.quote: for the first time in 4 years, an AMD-powered supercomputer is no longer at the top.
insights.locator: The addition of LineShine to the list also means

insights.claim: Italy's Eni HPC7, an AMD MI300A system in HPE Cray EX255a racks, took #6 at 571 PFLOPs with much lower power (8.7MW), effectively a smaller El Capitan.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Enterprise/energy-sector exascale adoption of AMD MI300A reference design.
insights.locator: Speaking of AMD systems, a new AMD system

insights.claim: AI hardware is starting to shed FP64 support to improve mixed-precision performance, and enormous unbenchmarked systems exist outside the Linpack/Top500 framework.
insights.speaker: Patrick Kennedy
insights.so_what: Top500 Linpack relevance is eroding as AI workloads prioritize mixed precision over FP64; rankings understate true compute.
insights.quote: the AI hardware is starting to shed FP64 support to further improve mixed-precision performance.
